meet-claude.instruction: Write a prompt that explores Claude's capabilities by asking it to do something creative AND analytical in the same response.
meet-claude.hint: Write a haiku about machine learning, then explain the scientific concept behind each line. For each line, include the specific ML term it references and why that concept matters.
meet-claude.successMessage: Claude excels at combining creative and analytical thinking — something most AI struggles with.

system-prompts.instruction: Write a system prompt that gives Claude a specific expertise, communication style, and set of rules to follow.
system-prompts.hint: You are a senior software architect with 20 years of experience. You communicate in clear, direct sentences — never more than 2 sentences per point. Always consider scalability, security, and maintainability. When asked about a technology choice, give your recommendation first, then explain why. Never say 'it depends' without following up with specific criteria for deciding.
system-prompts.goodExample: You are a financial advisor specializing in early-career professionals aged 22-30. Explain everything without jargon. Always give a concrete example with dollar amounts. Never recommend individual stocks. When someone asks 'should I...', give a clear yes/no with reasoning before presenting alternatives. Keep responses under 200 words.
system-prompts.successMessage: A great system prompt is like a job description: role, style, rules, and boundaries. This shapes every response Claude gives.

system-prompts.instruction: This system prompt is too vague: 'You are a helpful tutor. Be encouraging.' Make it specific and actionable.
system-prompts.hint: You are a math tutor for college calculus students. Start every explanation with the intuition behind the concept before showing formulas. Use real-world physics examples whenever possible. When a student gets something wrong, identify the specific misconception and address it directly. Never just give the answer — guide them to discover it through 2-3 leading questions. Celebrate progress with specific praise about what they did well.
system-prompts.successMessage: Vague instructions get vague behavior. Specific rules get consistent, predictable outputs.

chain-of-thought.instruction: Write a prompt that asks Claude to think step-by-step before giving a final answer. Include the phrase 'think step by step' or 'reason through this'.
chain-of-thought.placeholder: Think through this step by step...
chain-of-thought.hint: A company has 150 employees. They want to switch from individual software licenses ($50/month each) to a team plan ($35/month each, but requires minimum 100 seats). Think step by step: calculate the cost of each option, identify the break-even point, and recommend which plan to choose. Show your reasoning before the final recommendation.
chain-of-thought.successMessage: Chain-of-thought prompting dramatically improves accuracy on complex reasoning tasks. Always ask Claude to show its work.

few-shot-mastery.instruction: Write a few-shot prompt: give Claude 2-3 examples of input→output pairs, then ask it to handle a new input in the same pattern.
few-shot-mastery.hint:
Convert these customer complaints into professional, empathetic responses:

Complaint: 'Your app crashed and I lost all my work!'
Response: 'I completely understand how frustrating that must be — losing work is incredibly disruptive. Let me help you recover your data right away and investigate what caused the crash.'

Complaint: 'I've been on hold for 45 minutes!'
Response: 'I sincerely apologize for the long wait — your time is valuable and I know that was unacceptable. I'm here now and will resolve this as quickly as possible.'

Now convert this complaint: 'You charged me twice for the same order!'
few-shot-mastery.successMessage: Few-shot learning is one of the most powerful techniques. Examples teach Claude the exact pattern you want — no ambiguity.

temperature-lab.instruction: Write two versions of the same prompt — one that should produce consistent/factual output (low temperature) and one that should produce creative/varied output (high temperature). Label each.
temperature-lab.hint:
LOW TEMPERATURE (factual, consistent): 'List the 5 most common Python sorting algorithms, their time complexity in Big O notation, and when to use each. Be precise and technical.'

HIGH TEMPERATURE (creative, varied): 'Imagine each sorting algorithm is a character at a dinner party. Write a 3-sentence personality description for bubble sort, quicksort, and merge sort. Be creative and funny.'
temperature-lab.successMessage: Temperature control is about matching the task to the creativity level. Facts = low temp. Ideas = high temp.

tool-use-basics.instruction: Write a prompt that asks Claude to use a hypothetical tool. Describe the tool and tell Claude when and how to use it.
tool-use-basics.placeholder: You have access to a tool called...
tool-use-basics.hint: You have access to a tool called `search_database(query, filters)` that searches our customer database. When someone asks about a customer, use this tool to look them up before responding. Always search by email first, then by name if email isn't provided. After getting results, summarize the key account details: plan type, join date, and last activity.
tool-use-basics.successMessage: Tool use is the gateway to agents. Teaching Claude when and how to use tools turns it from a chatbot into a system.

context-window-explorer.instruction: Write a prompt that efficiently uses context window space. Ask Claude to analyze a document by telling it exactly what to focus on and ignore.
context-window-explorer.placeholder: Focus on X, skip Y, extract Z...
context-window-explorer.hint: I'm going to paste a 50-page contract below. I only need you to find: (1) payment terms and amounts, (2) termination clauses, (3) liability limitations. Skip all boilerplate definitions, party descriptions, and standard legal disclaimers. For each item found, give me the section number and a one-line plain-English summary.
context-window-explorer.successMessage: Context window management is a real skill. Telling Claude what to ignore is as important as telling it what to find.
